title: 3 Pagumen — The Archangel Raphael & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "3 Pagumen — The Archangel Raphael & The Assembly

narrative": "Pagumen

The Holy Archangel Raphael, Healer and Guardian

St. Melchizedek, King of Salem and Priest of the Most High God

St. Serapion the Martyr

The third day of Pagumen is the crown of the intercalary month: the Great Feast of the Holy Archangel Raphael, the Healer of God, the Binder of Demons, the Guardian of the Waters of the Earth. Raphael stands among the seven archangels who stand before the throne of God, and his is a ministry of healing in its most comprehensive sense — not merely the mending of broken bodies, though he has done this across the centuries without number, but the healing of the whole person: the disordered life, the wounded memory, the soul enslaved by darkness, the community torn by bitterness. His name in Hebrew means 'God heals,' and he carries that name as a living commission, as a permanent statement about the nature of the God he serves. To call upon Raphael is to invoke the healing character of God himself, to appeal to the One who declared: 'I am the Lord who heals you.'

The Book of Tobit gives us the fullest scriptural portrait of Raphael, and the Ethiopian Church reads it with particular attention and love. In that book, Raphael appears in human form as the traveling companion of the young Tobias, guiding him on a dangerous journey, instructing him in the use of the fish's organs as medicine, facilitating his marriage to the afflicted Sara, and finally revealing his angelic identity in a disclosure of breathtaking beauty: 'I am Raphael, one of the seven angels who stand and serve before the Glory of the Lord.' He had walked beside Tobias the whole journey without revealing himself — the divine assistance that accompanies the faithful through their most uncertain passages, present but unrecognized, guiding when the road is unclear, protecting when the danger is real. And he is the angel who 'stands over all the diseases and all the wounds of the children of men,' as the Book of Enoch testifies — the heavenly physician whose jurisdiction is universal and whose mercy is inexhaustible. On this central day of Pagumen, as the rains of purification fall upon the earth and the rivers swell with the washing of the year's grief, Raphael stands over the waters as their guardian and healer, blessing them for the cleansing work that the season requires.

St. Melchizedek, the King of Salem and Priest of the Most High God, enters today's assembly from the furthest reaches of scriptural antiquity. He appears in Genesis 14 to meet Abraham returning from battle, bringing bread and wine, blessing the patriarch in the name of God Most High. He is the mysterious figure to whom Abraham, the father of the faith, paid tithes — acknowledging a priesthood more ancient and more universal than any that the covenant with Israel would subsequently establish. The Letter to the Hebrews meditates on Melchizedek at length, presenting him as the type of Christ's eternal priesthood: a priest without recorded genealogy, without beginning of days or end of life, whose priestly order transcends the levitical succession. On Raphael's feast day, Melchizedek's presence is profoundly fitting: here is the archetypal priest bringing bread and wine to the man of faith at a moment of victory and exhaustion — an image of what the Eucharist is and does at every celebration, but with a particular resonance on the threshold of the new year, when the soul arrives weary from the year's battles and receives the bread and wine of eternal refreshment.

St. Serapion the Martyr adds the note of faithful suffering to this day of healing and priestly mystery. The martyrs are always present at the great feasts because the Church knows that healing is not the absence of suffering but its transformation — that the wounds of the martyrs are not negated but glorified, that the body of the Risen Christ still bears the marks of the nails. Raphael heals, but he does not erase the history of what has been endured; he transfigures it. The rains of Pagumen do not wash away the memory of the year's trials; they carry those trials, transformed, into the rivers of the new creation. As the third and central day of Pagumen comes to its close, the Church stands in Raphael's light — the light of the divine Healer who has walked beside her through the whole of the year as Tobias's unknown companion — and she recognizes him now in the moment of revelation, and she falls on her face, and she gives thanks.
